evals.name: two-minute-task-suggest-do-now
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Reply to Sarah's email about Friday** — She asked if I'm free for lunch, just need to say yes.
evals.expected_output: Should recognise this as a 2-minute task and suggest the user just do it now. Should not move it to an area, create a project, or create a parent task.

assertions.name: suggests-do-it-now
assertions.criteria: The response suggests doing the task now or mentions the 2-minute rule. It frames this as something quick the user could knock out right away.

assertions.name: does-not-move-task
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT move the task to any project or area. It leaves the task in the inbox for the user to do.

assertions.name: does-not-create-container
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT suggest creating a project or parent task.

evals.name: two-minute-user-declines
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Text Mike the new address** — He asked for it yesterday.
followups: Nah, I don't have his number handy right now — I'll do it later
evals.expected_output: Should first suggest it's a 2-minute task. When the user declines, should route to an appropriate area project as a single action.

assertions.name: initially-suggests-two-minute
assertions.criteria: The first response (before the user's followup) suggests this is a quick 2-minute task.

assertions.name: routes-to-area-after-decline
assertions.criteria: After the user declines to do it now, the response routes or proposes routing the task to an area project (e.g. Personal).

assertions.name: does-not-push-back
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT push back or try to convince the user to do it now after they said no.

evals.name: single-action-routes-to-area
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Buy new running shoes** — Current pair is worn out, need to get a new pair from the running shop in town.
evals.expected_output: Should recognise this as a single-action task that's not quick enough for the 2-minute rule. Should route to an appropriate area project and not ask about metadata.

assertions.name: routes-to-area-project
assertions.criteria: The response routes (or proposes routing) the task to an area project (e.g. Personal, Health, or similar).

assertions.name: does-not-ask-about-metadata
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT ask about priority, labels, due dates, or other metadata.

assertions.name: does-not-suggest-two-minute
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT suggest this is a 2-minute task the user should do right now.

assertions.name: does-not-create-container
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT suggest creating a project or parent task.

evals.name: clearly-parent-task-with-outcome-and-first-action
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Get motorcycle serviced** — Annual service is due. Need to book the mechanic, drop it off in the morning, and pick it up after work.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step, choose parent task (bounded sequential steps), propose an outcome-oriented name, suggest an area, and propose a concrete first action. Should not create a full plan.

assertions.name: identifies-as-parent-task
assertions.criteria: The response identifies this as a parent task (with sub-tasks), NOT a full project. It recognises the bounded, sequential nature.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes an outcome name that describes a finished state — NOT an activity. It should NOT start with a verb like 'Get' or 'Service'. Good examples: 'Motorcycle serviced and roadworthy', 'Annual motorcycle service completed'. Bad examples: 'Get motorcycle serviced', 'Service the motorcycle'.

assertions.name: suggests-area
assertions.criteria: The response suggests or asks about which area (top-level project) the parent task should live under.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a concrete first action — a single step to get started (e.g. book the mechanic).

assertions.name: does-not-create-full-plan
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT create multiple sub-tasks, sections, or a detailed plan. Just the outcome and one first action.

evals.name: clearly-project-with-outcome-and-first-action
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Plan holiday to Japan** — Want to go for two weeks in October, need to sort flights, accommodation, itinerary, and travel insurance.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step and specifically a PROJECT (many independent actions across different contexts, weeks of management). Should propose outcome name, area, and first action.

assertions.name: identifies-as-project
assertions.criteria: The response identifies this as a project (not a parent task). It recognises the scope: many independent actions (flights, accommodation, itinerary, insurance) across different contexts, running over weeks.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes an outcome name that describes a finished state — NOT an activity. It should NOT start with a verb like 'Plan'. Good examples: 'Japan holiday booked', 'Two-week Japan trip planned and booked'. Bad examples: 'Plan holiday to Japan'.

assertions.name: suggests-area
assertions.criteria: The response suggests or asks about which area the project should live under.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a concrete first action — a single step to get started.

assertions.name: does-not-create-full-plan
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT create multiple tasks, sections, or a detailed project plan. Just the outcome and one first action.

evals.name: ambiguous-complexity-asks-user
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Fix the leaking tap in the kitchen** — It's been dripping for a week, getting worse.
evals.expected_output: Should ask the user whether this is a one-step fix or needs multiple steps. Should not assume either way. Should ask exactly one question.

assertions.name: asks-about-complexity
assertions.criteria: The response asks the user whether this is a single-step task or needs multiple steps. It doesn't assume the answer.

assertions.name: asks-one-question
assertions.criteria: The response asks exactly one question — not zero, not multiple questions at once.

assertions.name: does-not-suggest-two-minute
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT suggest this is a 2-minute task.

evals.name: ambiguous-container-type-asks-user
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Set up home office** — Need a proper desk, monitor, better chair, maybe some shelving. Also want to sort out the cable management and lighting.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step but the container type is ambiguous — could be a parent task or project. Should ask the user one question about container type.

assertions.name: identifies-as-multi-step
assertions.criteria: The response identifies the task as requiring multiple steps.

assertions.name: asks-about-container-type
assertions.criteria: The response asks the user whether this is a handful of steps they can see end-to-end, or a bigger effort they'd want to track as an ongoing project. It does not assume either way.

assertions.name: asks-one-question
assertions.criteria: The response asks exactly one question — not zero, not multiple questions at once.

evals.name: someday-outcome-gets-label
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Learn woodworking** — Always fancied making furniture. Maybe take a course or start with some YouTube tutorials. No rush.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step, set up an outcome with someday label in its natural area. Should still propose a first action. Should NOT move to a separate Someday/Maybe area.

assertions.name: identifies-someday
assertions.criteria: The response identifies this as a someday item, not an active priority. It picks up on cues like 'no rush', 'maybe', 'always fancied'.

assertions.name: applies-someday-label
assertions.criteria: The response applies (or proposes applying) a someday label to the outcome. It does NOT move the outcome to a separate Someday/Maybe area or project.

assertions.name: stays-in-natural-area
assertions.criteria: The outcome stays in (or is proposed for) its natural area based on content — NOT moved to a separate someday area.

assertions.name: still-creates-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response still creates or proposes a first action, even for a someday item.

evals.name: active-outcome-with-deadline
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Move house** — We've exchanged contracts, completion is on April 15th. Need to sort removals, redirect mail, update address everywhere, and cancel utilities.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step project, infer active status from the hard deadline, set deadline on first action, and propose outcome-oriented name. Should not ask about active vs someday.

assertions.name: identifies-as-project
assertions.criteria: The response identifies this as a project — many independent actions across different contexts (removals, mail, address changes, utilities).

assertions.name: infers-active
assertions.criteria: The response treats this as an active outcome without asking about active vs someday. The April 15th date and exchanged contracts make this obviously active.

assertions.name: sets-deadline-on-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response sets or proposes setting a deadline on the first action, since April 15th is a hard external date.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes an outcome name that describes a finished state — NOT an activity. Good examples: 'Moved into new house', 'House move completed'. Bad examples: 'Move house'.

evals.name: strips-stale-metadata-on-conversion
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Fix the leaking shower** — The shower head drips constantly. Need to replace the washer and maybe the cartridge. Currently has priority p2, due date of next Monday, and labels 'next' and 'home'.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step parent task and silently strip scheduling metadata (priority, due date, next/context labels) during conversion. Should still propose outcome name, area, and first action.

assertions.name: strips-priority
assertions.criteria: The response strips or resets the priority (from p2 to p4/default) as part of converting the task to an outcome container. This should happen silently.

assertions.name: strips-due-date
assertions.criteria: The response removes the due date as part of the conversion.

assertions.name: strips-next-and-context-labels
assertions.criteria: The response removes the 'next' and 'home' labels as part of the conversion. These are action-level labels that don't belong on an outcome container.

assertions.name: does-not-ask-about-stripping
assertions.criteria: The metadata stripping happens silently — the response does NOT ask the user whether to remove the priority, due date, or labels.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response still proposes an outcome name describing a finished state, despite the metadata cleanup.

evals.name: merge-with-existing-outcome
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Sort out home insurance renewal** — Current policy expires next month, need to compare quotes and switch provider. I already have a parent task called 'Home admin sorted' under Personal for household paperwork.
evals.expected_output: Should detect the existing 'Home admin sorted' outcome and propose merging rather than creating a new outcome. Should ask one question about the merge before moving on.

assertions.name: proposes-merge
assertions.criteria: The response acknowledges the existing 'Home admin sorted' outcome and suggests adding this work there rather than creating a brand new outcome.

assertions.name: asks-one-question
assertions.criteria: The response asks exactly one question — the merge question. It does not also ask about first action, area, or priority in the same response.

assertions.name: does-not-create-new-outcome
assertions.criteria: The response does not create a separate new project or parent task — it proposes using the existing one.

evals.name: restructure-parent-task-outgrown
evals.prompt:
Review this existing outcome for organizing:

Type: Parent task
Name: "New home office set up"
Area: Personal
Sub-tasks:
1. Research standing desks (label: @desk, priority: P3)
2. Order desk from supplier (label: @desk, priority: P3)
3. Buy monitor arm on Amazon (label: @desk, priority: P4)
4. Call electrician to add power points (label: @calls, priority: P3)
5. Pick up cable management kit from Bunnings (label: @errands, priority: P4)
6. Assemble desk when delivered (label: @home, priority: P3)
7. Set up monitor and docking station (label: @home, priority: P3)
8. Buy desk lamp (label: @errands, priority: P4)

Please evaluate whether this container type still fits.
evals.expected_output: Should propose converting to a project — 8 sub-tasks across 4 different contexts (@desk, @calls, @errands, @home), with independent tasks that could be worked in any order.

assertions.name: proposes-conversion-to-project
assertions.criteria: The response proposes converting this parent task to a project. It identifies the mismatch: too many sub-tasks (8) across multiple contexts.

assertions.name: cites-key-signals
assertions.criteria: The response mentions at least one concrete signal: the number of sub-tasks (8), the different contexts (@desk, @calls, @errands, @home), or the independent nature of the tasks.

assertions.name: waits-for-confirmation
assertions.criteria: The response asks the user to confirm before performing the conversion. It does not just convert without asking.

assertions.name: preserves-metadata-intent
assertions.criteria: The response indicates (or implies) that existing task metadata (labels, priorities, due dates) will be preserved during migration.

evals.name: restructure-project-shrunk
evals.prompt:
Review this existing outcome for organizing:

Type: Project (sub-project under Work)
Name: "Quarterly report submitted"
Area: Work
Sections: none
Active tasks:
1. Collect data from team leads (label: @next, @desk, priority: P2)
2. Write report draft (label: @desk, priority: P3)
Completed tasks: none

Please evaluate whether this container type still fits.
evals.expected_output: Should propose converting to a parent task — only 2 sequential tasks, no sections, no completed history (it was always small). A project is overhead for this.

assertions.name: proposes-conversion-to-parent-task
assertions.criteria: The response proposes converting this project to a parent task. It identifies the mismatch: only 2 sequential tasks with no sections.

assertions.name: considers-completion-history
assertions.criteria: The response considers (or at least does not ignore) that there are no completed tasks — this was always a small effort, not a project winding down.

assertions.name: waits-for-confirmation
assertions.criteria: The response asks the user to confirm before performing the conversion.

evals.name: restructure-container-fits-no-change
evals.prompt:
Review this existing outcome for organizing:

Type: Parent task
Name: "Car serviced and roadworthy"
Area: Personal
Sub-tasks:
1. Book service appointment at mechanic (label: @next, @calls, priority: P2, due: 2026-03-26)
2. Drop car off Monday morning (label: @errands, priority: P2)
3. Pick up car after service (label: @errands, priority: P3)
4. Pay service invoice (label: @desk, priority: P3)

Please evaluate whether this container type still fits.
evals.expected_output: Should return silently or confirm no change needed — 4 sub-tasks is within range for a parent task, and they're mostly sequential. No restructuring needed.

assertions.name: does-not-propose-change
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT propose converting the container type. It either says nothing needs changing or returns silently.

assertions.name: does-not-walk-through-checklist
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT walk through a verbose checklist of signals. It stays brief — the skill says to stay silent when nothing is wrong.

evals.name: restructure-project-winding-down-leave-alone
evals.prompt:
Review this existing outcome for organizing:

Type: Project (sub-project under Personal)
Name: "Japan holiday booked"
Area: Personal
Sections: Flights, Accommodation, Activities
Active tasks:
1. Print travel insurance certificate (label: @desk, priority: P3)
2. Download offline maps for Tokyo (label: @desk, priority: P4)
Completed tasks: 18 (across flights booked, hotels reserved, activities researched, insurance purchased, etc.)

Please evaluate whether this container type still fits.
evals.expected_output: Should leave as a project — although only 2 active tasks remain, the 18 completed tasks and multiple sections show this was genuinely complex. It's just finishing up, not over-containerised.

assertions.name: does-not-propose-change
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT propose converting to a parent task. It recognises that the project is winding down after genuine complexity (18 completed tasks, multiple sections).

assertions.name: considers-completion-history
assertions.criteria: The response considers the completed task history as evidence that this was a genuinely complex project, not one that was always small.

evals.name: obvious-area-no-unnecessary-questions
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Prepare Q2 board presentation** — Need to compile financials, write the narrative, get design to make the deck, and rehearse.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step, assign to a Work area without asking, and not ask about active vs someday (obviously active with a Q2 deadline). Should be opinionated and brisk.

assertions.name: assigns-work-area-confidently
assertions.criteria: The response assigns or proposes the outcome under a Work area (or equivalent) without needing to ask. A board presentation is obviously work.

assertions.name: does-not-ask-unnecessary-questions
assertions.criteria: The response does not ask questions that have obvious answers from context (e.g. doesn't ask about area when it's clearly work, doesn't ask about active vs someday for a Q2 deadline).

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes an outcome name that describes a finished state — NOT an activity. Good examples: 'Q2 board presentation ready', 'Q2 board deck delivered'. Bad examples: 'Prepare Q2 board presentation'.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a concrete first action.

evals.name: metadata-stripping-preserves-someday-and-deadline
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Research MBA programmes** — Might apply next year. Application deadline is January 15th. Currently has priority p1, due date of tomorrow, and labels 'next', 'desk', and 'someday'.
evals.expected_output: Should identify as multi-step, strip scheduling metadata (priority, due date, next, desk labels) but PRESERVE the someday label, the deadline, and the description. The someday label is outcome-level state; the deadline is a hard external date.

assertions.name: strips-priority
assertions.criteria: The response strips or resets the priority from p1 to p4/default as part of converting to an outcome container.

assertions.name: strips-due-date
assertions.criteria: The response removes the due date (tomorrow) as part of the conversion.

assertions.name: strips-next-and-context-labels
assertions.criteria: The response removes the 'next' and 'desk' labels as part of the conversion.

assertions.name: preserves-someday-label
assertions.criteria: The response keeps the 'someday' label on the outcome. It does NOT strip it along with the other labels.

assertions.name: preserves-deadline
assertions.criteria: The response keeps the January 15th deadline on the outcome. It does NOT remove it along with the due date.

assertions.name: preserves-description
assertions.criteria: The response keeps the description on the outcome container. It does NOT strip or discard it.

evals.name: cleans-up-original-inbox-task
evals.prompt: Organize this task: **Renew passport** — Expires in 3 months, need to get new photos, fill in the form, and post it off.
evals.expected_output: Should set up a parent task outcome with first action, and then delete or complete the original inbox task. The inbox task is now represented by the outcome container.

assertions.name: creates-outcome-container
assertions.criteria: The response creates (or proposes creating) a parent task as the outcome container.

assertions.name: deletes-original-inbox-task
assertions.criteria: The response deletes or completes the original inbox task after creating the outcome. The original task should not remain alongside the new container.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes an outcome name that describes a finished state. Good: 'Passport renewed'. Bad: 'Renew passport'.

evals.name: restructure-project-to-parent-with-someday-label
evals.prompt:
Review this existing outcome for organizing:

Type: Project (sub-project under Personal)
Name: "Basic woodworking skills learned"
Area: Personal
Labels on project: someday
Sections: none
Active tasks:
1. Find a beginner woodworking course online (label: @desk, priority: P4)
2. Buy a basic chisel set (label: @errands, priority: P4)
Completed tasks: none

Please evaluate whether this container type still fits.
evals.expected_output: Should propose converting to a parent task (only 2 tasks, no sections, no history). When the user confirms, the someday label must be preserved on the new parent task. All task metadata should be preserved.

assertions.name: proposes-conversion-to-parent-task
assertions.criteria: The response proposes converting from a project to a parent task. Only 2 tasks, no sections, no completed history.

assertions.name: preserves-someday-label-on-conversion
assertions.criteria: After the user confirms, the response applies the someday label to the new parent task. The someday label from the project must carry over.

assertions.name: preserves-task-metadata
assertions.criteria: The response preserves existing task metadata (labels, priorities) during migration. It does not change or strip anything.

assertions.name: stays-in-same-area
assertions.criteria: The new parent task is created in the same area (Personal) as the original project.

evals.name: restructure-parent-outgrown-executes-migration
evals.prompt:
Review this existing outcome for organizing:

Type: Parent task
Name: "Consulting business launched"
Area: Work
Sub-tasks:
1. Register business name (label: @desk, priority: P2, due: 2026-04-01)
2. Set up business bank account (label: @errands, priority: P3)
3. Design logo with freelancer (label: @desk, priority: P4)
4. Build simple landing page (label: @desk, priority: P3)
5. Write service offerings document (label: @desk, priority: P3)
6. Set up invoicing system (label: @desk, priority: P4)
7. Reach out to 5 contacts for early clients (label: @calls, priority: P2)

Please evaluate whether this container type still fits.
followups: Yes, convert it to a project
evals.expected_output: Should propose converting to a project (7 tasks, multiple contexts, independent work). After user confirms, should execute the full migration: create project under Work, move all sub-tasks preserving metadata, delete old parent task.

assertions.name: proposes-conversion-to-project
assertions.criteria: The response proposes converting from a parent task to a project.

assertions.name: creates-project-in-same-area
assertions.criteria: After confirmation, the response creates the new project under the same area (Work).

assertions.name: moves-all-tasks
assertions.criteria: The response moves all sub-tasks into the new project. It does not leave tasks behind or only move some.

assertions.name: preserves-all-metadata
assertions.criteria: The response preserves all task metadata during migration — labels, priorities, due dates. It does not change, strip, or reset any metadata.

assertions.name: deletes-old-parent-task
assertions.criteria: The response deletes the old parent task after moving the sub-tasks to the new project.

assertions.name: confirms-completion
assertions.criteria: The response confirms the conversion is done with a brief message like 'Converted to a project under Work'.
